Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Analytic Philosophy of Mind

Christian List

b. 1978

Free will, agency, and non-reductive levels in a naturalistic philosophy of mind.

The central result is this: List’s view is not Cartesian substance dualism, not reductive materialism, and not standard non-reductive physicalism all the way down. It is best understood as a two-layer or two-track position. For intentional agency, belief, desire, choice, and mental causation, List defends a naturalistic, non-reductive, higher-level realism: minds and agents are real because they figure indispensably and causally in the best explanatory frameworks of psychology, decision theory, and the human sciences. For phenomenal consciousness, however, List becomes more radical: subjective experience is irreducibly first-personal, does not supervene on third-personal physical facts, and requires a departure from the ordinary “one objective world” picture.

That gives us something close to a secularized soul-theory, but only if “soul” is understood as first-personal subjectivity plus agential personhood, not as a separable immortal substance. If one means by “soul” an immaterial substance capable of surviving bodily death, List does not supply that doctrine. If one means the irreducible first-personal center of experience and responsibility, List gives one of the more interesting contemporary analytic frameworks for reconstructing it.

1. Corpus and orientation

Christian List is a philosopher and decision theorist whose work spans social choice theory, judgment aggregation, group agency, philosophy of mind, philosophy of action, free will, and consciousness. His institutional profile describes recent work on free will, mental causation, levels, and consciousness, alongside group agency and social ontology. His public LMU presentation of the free-will project explicitly frames free will, consciousness, and intentionality as central to understanding the distinctiveness of human action and decision within a scientific worldview.

The most important texts for extracting a philosophy of mind are these: “A Naturalistic Case for Free Will,” “Mental Causation and the Metaphysics of Mind” with Peter Menzies, “Levels of Description and Levels of Reality,” “The Many-Worlds Theory of Consciousness,” “The First-Person Argument against Physicalism,” “The Quadrilemma of Consciousness,” “What Is It Like to Be a Group Agent?” with Philip Pettit, and more recent work on decision theory, AI free will, and the relation between consciousness and quantum mechanics. The 2026 preprint with DeBrota extends his anti-objectivist line, arguing that both consciousness and quantum mechanics challenge a classical objectivist worldview organized around non-relationalism, non-fragmentation, and one world.

One caution is essential. List’s “mind” is not a single metaphysical item. He treats intentionality, agency, and free will differently from phenomenal consciousness. Beliefs, desires, preferences, intentions, and choices can be real higher-level phenomena that supervene on physical states without being reducible to them. But subjective experience, on his argument, cannot be captured by the same non-reductive physicalist template. List explicitly says that supervenience on physical facts may be plausible for many ordinary mental properties such as beliefs, desires, and preferences, while consciousness is different.

So the extracted philosophy of mind must not flatten List into either “emergent physicalist” or “dualist.” He is more complex: non-reductive realist about the intentional mind, anti-physicalist about phenomenal consciousness, and pluralist or many-worlds realist about first-person subjectivity.

2. List’s basic metaphysical grammar: levels, facts, worlds

List’s philosophy of mind begins with his theory of levels. He works with a fact-based and world-based ontology. A “world at a level” is the totality of what is the case at that level. Physical worlds contain physical facts; biological worlds contain biological facts; psychological worlds contain psychological facts; social worlds contain social facts. His levels framework is designed to analyze supervenience, reduction, grounding, constitution, determinism, indexicality, consciousness, and the relation between positive and normative facts.

This is not merely linguistic pluralism. List is not saying that there are only different vocabularies for one set of fundamental facts. He distinguishes levels of description from levels of reality. Physics may speak in terms of particles or fields, biology in terms of cells and organisms, psychology in terms of mental states, intentionality, and cognition, and the social sciences in terms of institutions and norms. The important question is whether higher-level facts are reducible to lower-level facts or whether they are emergent and real in their own right.

Supervenience is central. A higher-level fact can depend on lower-level facts in such a way that no higher-level difference occurs without some lower-level difference. But supervenience need not imply reduction. A psychological state can be multiply realizable: the same mental state may be realized by many distinct physical configurations. List’s framework explicitly allows psychological-level worlds to include mental facts that supervene on physical facts without themselves being physical facts, and he uses the brain-mind relation as a paradigm of multiple realizability.

This gives us the first major principle of a Listian philosophy of mind:

Mental reality should be understood level-relatively. A mind is not to be identified with one microphysical arrangement. It is a real higher-level pattern, role, or structure that can be realized in different lower-level ways.

This is not eliminativism. It is not the claim that mental states are convenient fictions. On the contrary, the higher-level sciences disclose real patterns that are not captured by the physical level alone. Mental states can be causally and explanatorily indispensable even if they are physically realized.

3. The intentional mind: belief, desire, preference, intention

The first part of List’s philosophy of mind concerns intentional agency. Here he is broadly naturalistic and non-reductive. Human beings are intelligible as agents because we explain their behavior by attributing goals, beliefs, desires, preferences, intentions, and responsiveness to reasons. He argues that the human and social sciences support a non-reductionistic picture of humans as agents with goals, beliefs, desires, and other intentional features, and that these features emerge from, or supervene on, physical properties without being reducible to them.

List’s appeal to the “intentional stance” is important. We understand and predict human behavior by treating people as agents who have goals, respond rationally to their circumstances, and make choices. This is not merely folk psychology in a dismissive sense. It is an indispensable explanatory stance used across psychology, economics, decision theory, and social science.

This is where List’s philosophy of mind is closest to Daniel Dennett, but with an important realist difference. Dennett sometimes sounds as though intentionality is a useful stance rather than a metaphysically robust feature of the world. List’s position is more realist: if an explanatory framework is indispensable, systematically successful, and causally informative, then the entities and properties it posits have a claim to reality at their own level.

The intentional mind, then, is not a ghost inside the machine. It is a real higher-level organization of the person as a rational, goal-directed, option-sensitive system. Intentionality belongs to the psychological and agential level, not to microphysics. It is therefore a mistake to ask physics alone to find beliefs and intentions, just as it would be a mistake to ask particle physics alone to find inflation, constitutions, marriages, or obligations.

This yields a second principle:

Intentional mental states are higher-level, multiply realizable, causally relevant states of agents. Their reality is secured by their role in the best explanations of action, not by their reducibility to neural states.

4. Mental causation: the mind as causally efficacious

The familiar objection to non-reductive physicalism is Jaegwon Kim’s causal exclusion problem. If every physical event has a sufficient physical cause, and if mental properties are not identical to physical properties, then what causal work is left for the mental? Mental causes seem either redundant or epiphenomenal.

List and Peter Menzies respond by using a difference-making account of causation. They argue that special-science properties may be non-identical with physical properties because they are multiply realizable, may supervene on physical properties, and may nonetheless be genuine causes and effects. Their account is intended to secure “causal autonomy” for the special sciences.

Their key move is proportionality. A cause should be specified at the right level of generality. Sometimes the best cause of an effect is not the highly specific neural or microphysical state, but the higher-level mental property that captures exactly the difference-making condition. Their monkey example illustrates this: the monkey’s intention to reach for something can be the proportional cause of the reaching action, even though that intention is physically realized by some neural state. The neural realizer may be too specific, because many different neural states could realize the same intention and lead to the same kind of behavior.

This is crucial for the philosophy of mind. The mind does not merely float above the physical as an inert description. Mental properties can be causes precisely because they identify the right difference-making pattern. In many cases, the higher-level property is more causally explanatory than the lower-level realizer. List and Menzies argue that higher-level causal relations are often realization-insensitive, and that mental properties can be proportional difference-making causes.

This gives us a third principle:

Mental causation is real when mental properties are the proportional difference-makers for behavior. The fact that a mental state is physically realized does not deprive it of causal power.

The theological significance is immediate, though indirect. A soul-theory often wants to say that persons act, choose, sin, repent, intend, and love—not merely that brains undergo events. List’s theory gives a secular metaphysical basis for person-level causation. It does not require an immaterial soul-substance, but it does support the claim that agency and intention are not illusory byproducts of neural machinery.

5. Free will: List’s higher-level compatibilist libertarianism

List’s free-will theory is among the clearest places where his philosophy of mind becomes an anthropology. In “A Naturalistic Case for Free Will,” he defines free will roughly as the capacity to choose and control one’s actions, and he analyzes it through three conditions: intentional agency, alternative possibilities, and mental causation. He concedes that these conditions are required for free will and argues that, jointly, they are sufficient. He then defends the claim that they are emergent higher-level phenomena.

His conclusion is striking: people have intentional agency, alternative possibilities, and mental causation at the agential level. Free will is therefore real as a higher-level phenomenon. He calls the view “compatibilist libertarianism”: libertarian because it preserves genuine alternative possibilities and agential indeterminism; compatibilist because this can be compatible with determinism at the fundamental physical level.

This is a subtle position. Standard compatibilism says that free will is compatible with determinism because freedom does not require alternative possibilities in the strong libertarian sense. Standard libertarianism says that free will requires alternative possibilities and is incompatible with determinism. List splits the issue by level. At the level of fundamental physics, determinism may hold. At the level of agency, the best explanatory models may still be indeterministic: multiple actions are genuinely open to the agent in the decision-theoretic sense.

A recent German article sharpens this point. List argues against a purely reason-based compatibilism that tries to retain responsibility without genuine alternatives. His objection is that reason-based explanations presuppose an agent standing before several action possibilities, with reasons for and against them. Decision-theoretic explanations themselves presuppose alternatives. He further insists that, if such explanations are to be true rather than merely useful fictions, the alternatives must be ontic at the relevant level, not merely imagined. This does not require physical-level indeterminism; it requires real agential possibilities.

This matters greatly for any “soul” reconstruction. List gives us a philosophy of the person as a locus of choice. A person is not merely a place where events happen. A person is an agent whose intentional states and reasons make a causal difference, and whose decision situation contains real alternatives at the agential level.

The fourth principle is therefore:

Free will is a higher-level property of agents: it consists in intentional agency, real agential alternatives, and mental causal control. It need not be visible from the physical level to be real.

This also explains why List’s view is attractive for theology, law, and ethics. It preserves responsibility without requiring metaphysical randomness in the brain. It gives a naturalistic account of why guilt, merit, blame, repentance, and deliberation are not empty practices. Yet it does not ground freedom in an immaterial soul. Freedom belongs to agents as higher-level systems.

6. Decision theory and the presupposition of free will

List’s connection between decision theory and free will is especially important. Decision theory does not merely describe behavior from the outside; it models agents as facing options, having preferences, weighing reasons, and choosing. List argues that the explanatory frameworks used in the sciences of human behavior presuppose intentional agency, choice, and causal control. In his 2025 work on AI free will, he writes that we should not look for some mysterious intrinsic property, indeterministic algorithm, or sheer unpredictability. Instead, we should ask whether the best explanatory reasons support viewing a system as an intentional agent with choice and control.

That same paper generalizes the point to artificial systems and group agents. If the explanatory logic of choice-making applies to a system, the system may satisfy a condition relevant to free will and even moral responsibility. List notes analogous arguments for group and corporate agents, arguing that free will is not restricted in principle to biological agents.

This reveals an important distinction. For List, agency is not identical to consciousness. A system can be an agent if it is appropriately modeled as choosing, acting for reasons, and exercising control. Whether it has phenomenal consciousness is a further question. This distinction will become central when we turn to group minds and theology.

A fifth principle follows:

Agency is functionally and explanatorily grounded; phenomenal consciousness is not thereby guaranteed. There can be agents without inner subjective life, or at least without the kind of phenomenal consciousness humans possess.

This is a significant departure from many theological and personalist traditions, which often tie personhood, agency, consciousness, and soul tightly together. List’s framework allows them to come apart.

7. Consciousness: the break with physicalism

List’s most radical philosophy of mind appears in his work on subjective consciousness. He does not merely repeat the standard “hard problem” argument. His distinctive argument turns on first-person facts.

In “The First-Person Argument against Physicalism,” he argues that subjective experience facts are first-personal or indexical; physical facts, conventionally understood, are third-personal or non-indexical; and first-personal/indexical facts do not supervene on third-personal/non-indexical facts. Therefore, some subjective-experience facts do not supervene on physical facts.

The key is not simply that consciousness feels mysterious. It is that the fact “this experience is mine,” or “I am the subject of this experience,” is not captured by a complete third-person description of the physical world. A full objective inventory may say that Christian List has such-and-such neural states, that someone is experiencing pain, or that a certain organism is conscious. But that does not yield the first-personal fact that this experience is present to me.

List treats subjective experience as a datum. He defends realism about subjective experience and argues that theories of mind must accommodate genuinely first-personal facts. He concludes that a physicalist ontology is incomplete and that a richer ontology is needed.

This marks a departure from his treatment of intentional states. Beliefs and desires may be higher-level states that supervene on physical facts. Conscious experience, by contrast, does not supervene on physical facts if physical facts are construed as third-personal. The problem is not only reducibility; it is the inability of the third-personal inventory to determine the first-personal center.

Here the sixth principle emerges:

Phenomenal consciousness consists in irreducibly first-personal facts. These facts are not determined by the totality of third-person physical facts.

This is the closest List comes to a philosophical reconstruction of what theologians often mean by the soul as inwardness: the subject is not exhausted by the externally observable organism. But the resemblance must be handled carefully. List is not saying there is an immaterial soul-stuff. He is saying that first-personal facts are ontologically indispensable.

8. The “first-person argument” and the limits of substance dualism

One of the most theologically important features of List’s work is that his argument does not simply vindicate old-fashioned substance dualism. In fact, he explicitly notes a problem for mainstream dualism and related views. He argues that many standard positions—dualism, double-aspect theory, Russellian monism, neutral monism—still operate within a one-world, third-personal, “view from nowhere” framework.

List draws on Thomas Nagel’s point that even noncorporeal substance dualism may fail if it merely postulates an additional substance whose states are described objectively. The problem remains: why are those states underivatively mine?

This is a major correction to simplistic theological uses of anti-physicalist arguments. It is not enough to say, “Consciousness is not physical, therefore there is a soul.” For List, the deeper issue is not whether the bearer of consciousness is physical or nonphysical. The deeper issue is whether the theory can account for first-personal presence. A nonphysical ego described from the outside is still a third-personal item. It does not automatically explain subjectivity.

So a Listian critique of physicalism is also a critique of crude soul-substance metaphysics. The soul, if retained, must not be treated as merely another object in the world. It must be understood as a first-personal center or locus of subjectivity.

This produces the seventh principle:

A theory of the soul cannot succeed merely by adding a nonphysical substance to the world. It must explain, or at least accommodate, irreducible first-personal presence.

The theological payoff is substantial. List’s work pushes theology away from “ghost in the machine” dualism and toward a more phenomenological or first-personal account of ensouled existence.

9. The quadrilemma of consciousness

List’s “Quadrilemma of Consciousness” develops the same thought in a more general metaphysical form. He argues that no theory can simultaneously preserve four claims: first-person realism, non-solipsism, non-fragmentation, and one world. Any three can be retained, but not all four.

The four claims can be summarized as follows. First-person realism says first-person facts are real and irreducible. Non-solipsism says there are other conscious subjects besides me. Non-fragmentation says reality is not internally fragmented into mutually incompatible domains. One-world says there is only one world containing all the facts. The problem is that first-person facts for different subjects are not compossible as first-person facts in one non-fragmented world. My first-person fact “I am this subject” and your first-person fact “I am that subject” cannot both be present in the same first-personal way within a single centered world.

List argues that mainstream physicalists, dualists, Russellian monists, neutral monists, and double-aspect theorists usually retain the single objective world picture and therefore must, in effect, give up full first-person realism. His preferred route is to give up one-world metaphysics. He proposes a many-worlds theory in which there are first-personally centered worlds: all are real, but only one is present to each subject.

This is not the many-worlds interpretation of quantum mechanics. List explicitly separates his many-worlds theory of consciousness from quantum mechanics, although he later explores analogies between consciousness and quantum theory. The many worlds are first-personally centered worlds, not branching physical universes.

The eighth principle is therefore:

To preserve first-person realism and non-solipsism, we may need to abandon the idea that all facts belong to a single uncentered objective world.

This is perhaps the most metaphysically daring part of List’s thought. It is also the part most relevant to theological reflection on divine omniscience, creation, and the unity of the world.

10. The many-worlds theory of consciousness

In “The Many-Worlds Theory of Consciousness,” List proposes that each conscious subject is associated with a first-personally centered world. The shared third-personal world is supervenient on, or abstracted from, these first-personally centered worlds. All such centered worlds are real, but only one is present to any given subject.

The basic idea is that the locus of each subject’s experiences is a subject-specific first-personally centered world. Distinct subjects have distinct first-personal realizers, while sharing a third-personal world. Formally, List models a first-personally centered world as something like an ordered pair encoding both a third-personal world and a locus of subjectivity. Such a world includes both third-personal and first-personal facts.

This produces a layered ontology. At the first-personal level, facts are centered: they are facts as present to a subject. At the third-personal level, the shared world abstracts away from which subject is the locus of presence. The same physical organisms and environments may be shared third-personally, while first-personal facts differ across centered worlds.

List’s theory explains why other people’s experiences are first-personally inaccessible. My references to your experiences are references to facts located in another first-personally centered world, one not present to me. This avoids solipsism because the other centered worlds are real. But it also avoids pretending that your pain is present to me in the same first-personal way my pain is.

The result is a radical form of first-person realism:

Conscious subjects do not merely occupy different locations in one objective world. Each subject is associated with a first-personally centered world in which that subject’s experiences are present.

This is where List’s thought most strongly invites comparison with soul-language. A “soul,” in one reconstructed sense, could mean the first-personal locus of a centered world. But again, this must not be confused with an immaterial Cartesian substance. The locus of subjectivity is not necessarily a detachable entity. It is the metaphysical center of experiential presence.

11. First-person worlds, personal identity, and the self

List’s theory raises an obvious question: what is the self? Is the self the organism, the brain, the psychological continuity relation, the locus of subjectivity, or the first-personal world?

List is careful. The organism exists in the shared third-personal world. The psychological agent exists at the higher agential level. The locus of subjectivity is what centers a first-personal world. These are related but not identical.

In later discussion, List notes that one could formulate the theory by taking first-personally centered worlds as primitive and then introducing an equivalence relation to encode personal identity across them. That suggests that personal identity is not simply reducible to bodily identity or neural continuity within the third-personal world. It must also be sensitive to the structure of first-personal centeredness.

However, List does not provide a traditional doctrine of the enduring soul. He does not argue that the locus of subjectivity survives death, migrates, reincarnates, or exists independently of embodiment. His framework leaves open questions about persistence, but it does not settle them in a theological direction.

A cautious Listian account of the self would therefore say:

The self is not identical with the body as described from nowhere. It is the embodied higher-level agent as centered by first-personal subjectivity. But the theory does not by itself establish disembodied survival.

This is highly relevant to theology. It is friendly to the idea that persons are more than bodies externally described. It is not, by itself, a proof of immortality.

12. Awareness, experience, and group minds

List’s work with Philip Pettit on group agency and group consciousness introduces another important distinction. Groups can be agents. Corporations, courts, NGOs, states, and other organized collectives can have purposes, intentions, and rational structures. But group agency does not automatically entail phenomenal consciousness.

List and Pettit distinguish consciousness as awareness from consciousness as experience. Awareness can be treated functionally and third-personally: a system monitors its environment, processes information, updates its states, and responds. Experience, by contrast, is first-personal and phenomenal: there is something it is like for the subject.

They argue that group agents can have awareness in the functional sense. Groups can collect information, deliberate, respond to threats, and act through institutional mechanisms. But they are skeptical that group agents have phenomenal consciousness. Using integrated information theory as one possible criterion, they suggest that group agents would likely have very low integrated information and therefore little or no phenomenal consciousness. Their answer to “what is it like to be a group agent?” is close to: nothing, or almost nothing.

This matters for moral status. List and Pettit suggest that if phenomenal consciousness is required for non-derivative moral significance, humans and perhaps some animals have it, while group agents do not. Groups may have derivative rights or responsibilities, but not the same kind of intrinsic moral standing as conscious subjects.

This gives us a ninth principle:

Agency, awareness, and phenomenal consciousness are distinct. A system may be an agent without being a phenomenal subject.

The theological implications are subtle. Churches, nations, corporations, and communities may be real agents. They can act, intend, deliberate, sin, repent, or be responsible in some institutional sense. But they need not have a collective soul or collective phenomenal interiority. This is useful for ecclesiology and political theology: “the body” of a community can be an agent without being a literal conscious subject.

13. AI, non-human agents, and the separability of soul and agency

List’s 2025 work on AI free will reinforces the distinction between agency and consciousness. He argues that the question “Can AI systems have free will?” should not be answered by searching for metaphysical mystery, algorithmic indeterminism, or unpredictability. It should be answered by asking whether the system is appropriately treated as an intentional agent with choice and control under the best explanatory framework.

If an artificial system satisfies the explanatory conditions of agency, it may have free will in the relevant pragmatic or diagnostic sense. List extends similar reasoning to corporations and group agents. But again, agency does not automatically imply phenomenal consciousness. AI might be an intentional agent in the explanatory sense without having first-personal experience.

This is important for any theological anthropology drawn from List. The soul cannot simply be identified with agency, because List’s framework allows non-human, artificial, and collective agents. Nor can it simply be identified with rational decision-making. If “soul” is to pick out what is distinctively inward or first-personal, it must be tied to phenomenal subjectivity rather than merely agency.

So a Listian “soul” concept would likely have two components:

First, an agential component: the person as a higher-level intentional agent with choices, reasons, and causal control.

Second, a phenomenal component: the person as a first-personal center of experience.

Only the second component captures inward subjectivity. The first captures responsibility and rational action. A full personal anthropology needs both.

14. Theological anthropology: why the soul question is not a distraction

The theological question is not accidental. Theological anthropology has always had to ask what a person is, what consciousness is, how the soul relates to the body, whether freedom is real, and what grounds moral responsibility. Contemporary theological discussion is not monolithic: Christian anthropology includes soul-body dualism, hylomorphism, materialism, and other alternatives. The St Andrews Encyclopedia of Theology notes that “mind” can mean a substantial individual, goal-directedness, or awareness; that consciousness may refer to awareness or subjective experience; and that Christian sources have supported a range of models, including embodied but not necessarily body-identical accounts of soul.

List’s work should therefore not be filed away as merely secular philosophy. It bears directly on theological anthropology, especially in five areas: the soul-body relation, moral responsibility, divine knowledge, the unity of creation, and corporate or ecclesial agency.

First, List undermines reductive materialism. If consciousness includes irreducibly first-personal facts, then the human person cannot be fully captured by a third-personal physical inventory. This supports theological resistance to reducing persons to biological machines.

Second, List also undermines crude substance dualism. A nonphysical substance described from the outside does not by itself explain why an experience is mine. Theology must do better than adding a ghostly object to the furniture of the world.

Third, List supports a robust account of responsibility. His free-will theory preserves intentional agency, alternatives, and mental causation. This aligns with theological practices of confession, accountability, repentance, and moral formation, though List gives a naturalistic rather than explicitly theological grounding.

Fourth, List’s many-worlds theory raises questions for divine omniscience. If first-personal facts are real, then a complete divine knowledge cannot be merely an impersonal third-person catalogue. A God who knows all truths would have to know first-personal truths as first-personal truths, not merely as detached facts about which organism has which neural state. This is an inference from List’s argument, not a doctrine List himself develops.

Fifth, List’s work on group agency can illuminate theological claims about corporate bodies: churches, nations, institutions, and communities can be agents in a meaningful sense. But his skepticism about group phenomenal consciousness warns against treating corporate agents as literally possessing a collective inner life.

15. Soul as substance, soul as form, soul as first-person center

Classical theology offers several models of the soul. A Cartesian model treats the soul as an immaterial thinking substance. A hylomorphic model treats the soul as the form or organizing principle of the living body. A materialist Christian model may identify persons with organisms while preserving resurrection, agency, and divine relation. Other models move toward idealism, panpsychism, or participatory metaphysics. The contemporary theological literature recognizes this diversity rather than reducing Christianity to one doctrine.

Where does List fit?

He does not fit Cartesian dualism. His first-person argument pressures both physicalism and standard dualism. If the soul is merely another object, even a nonphysical one, the first-person problem remains.

He is closer to hylomorphism in his treatment of agency. The person is an organized higher-level system whose agency is not reducible to matter but is embodied and realized in it. Mental causation, intentionality, and free will are properties of the whole agent, not interventions by a separate ghost.

But his consciousness theory goes beyond standard hylomorphism. First-person centered worlds are not simply biological forms. They are metaphysical structures of subjectivity. A Listian “soul,” if we use the term, would be the first-personal locus of experiential presence, integrated with the higher-level embodied agent.

The most faithful reconstruction is therefore:

The soul is not an extra substance behind the person. It is the person as irreducibly first-personal and agential: a centered subject of experience and a higher-level agent capable of intentional choice.

This is not something List states as theology. It is a philosophical-theological extraction from his work.

16. Divine omniscience and the “view from nowhere”

List repeatedly criticizes the “Olympian” or third-personal view from nowhere. A complete third-person description of the world omits what is present from a first-person point of view. This has a serious theological implication.

Classical theology often imagines God as having a complete “God’s-eye view.” If that phrase means a maximally complete detached third-person inventory, then List’s argument suggests it is incomplete. First-person facts are not reducible to such an inventory. Therefore, divine omniscience, if it is to be complete, must include knowledge of first-personal facts.

This does not mean God must be identical with every subject, nor does it entail pantheism. It means that a theology of omniscience must distinguish between knowing that someone is in pain and knowing the first-personal character of pain as present to that subject. The divine knowledge of creatures cannot be merely observational if first-personal facts are real.

There are several possible theological developments. One could say God knows each subject’s first-personal world without collapsing it into God’s own subjectivity. One could say creaturely first-personal facts are transparent to God by divine omniscience. One could develop a participatory or incarnational account in which God’s knowledge includes creaturely interiority. List does not make these moves, but his framework forces the question.

The theological danger is equally clear: if one insists that reality is exhausted by a single objective world known from nowhere, one may inadvertently reproduce the very objectivism List argues cannot accommodate consciousness.

17. Creation and many first-person worlds

List’s many-worlds theory might appear to conflict with theological insistence on one creation. But the conflict depends on what “one world” means.

List does not deny that there is a shared third-personal world. His view says that the shared third-personal world is less fine-grained than the first-personally centered worlds. The same bodies, environments, and events can be part of a common third-personal structure while each subject has a distinct first-personal center.

A theologian could therefore reconcile List with “one creation” by saying: creation is one at the shared third-personal level, but plural in its first-personal centers. There is one created order, but many irreducible centers of creaturely presence.

The deeper issue is whether theology allows the reality of perspectives to be ontologically basic. Many theological traditions do: personalist, phenomenological, Augustinian, and some Eastern Christian approaches often treat inwardness, prayer, and personal encounter as irreducible. List gives these intuitions analytic form.

However, some theological metaphysics may resist List’s abandonment of one-world ontology. If divine creation is understood as one fully determinate, non-indexical totality, List’s many-worlds theory is disruptive. If creation is understood as a communion of subjects within a shared order, his theory may be more congenial.

18. Death, afterlife, and resurrection

List’s work does not establish an afterlife. This should be said plainly.

The non-supervenience of first-person facts on physical facts does not imply that subjectivity continues after bodily death. Nor does the reality of higher-level agency imply survival. List’s framework leaves room for a distinction between the organism, the agent, and the locus of subjectivity, but it does not supply a theological account of resurrection, immortality, reincarnation, or disembodied persistence.

That said, his theory does undermine a common argument against afterlife belief: the claim that a complete physical description of the body exhausts the person. If List is right, the physical description is not complete with respect to first-personal subjectivity. This does not prove survival, but it weakens reductive arguments that treat survival as obviously impossible because persons are nothing but bodies externally described.

A theological doctrine of resurrection would need additional premises: divine power, divine memory or preservation, continuity of personal identity, and perhaps re-centering of first-personal subjectivity in a transformed bodily life. List’s framework might help articulate what must be preserved—the first-personal center and agential identity—but it does not show that it is preserved.

So the careful conclusion is:

List makes room for a non-reductive account of personal subjectivity, but he does not provide a doctrine of immortality. Theological survival claims require further theological premises.

19. Non-English and non-Anglo reception

The non-English reception reveals useful pressure points, especially around free will.

A Spanish critique by J. P. Z. Bonilla, “Los niveles emergentes no salvan el libre albedrío,” challenges List’s appeal to emergent causal levels. According to its summary, the critique argues that emergent intentional action does not by itself imply freedom; that causality cannot simply be compartmentalized into a single level; and that distinguishing levels does not secure alternative possibilities if none exist at the fundamental level. This is an important objection because it targets the exact hinge of List’s compatibilist libertarianism: the claim that agential-level alternatives can be real even if fundamental physics is deterministic.

A French-hosted review in Œconomia, by Kevin Leportier, presents List’s book as arguing from the social sciences: economists and other social scientists explain behavior by presupposing intentions, alternatives, and control. The review also raises doubts about whether multiple realizability and higher-level causation are enough to secure free will, and questions whether decision theory truly presupposes agential indeterminism.

The German discussion is especially valuable because List himself has written in German on free will and alternatives. In “Willensfreiheit ohne Handlungsalternativen?” he argues that reason-based and decision-theoretic explanations presuppose real alternatives at the agential level. This shows that List is aware of the compatibilist attempt to detach responsibility from alternatives and explicitly resists it.

A German Catholic review of the German edition of his free-will book presents List’s view as defending free will in a gradual or relative form and as treating willing action as a higher and more complex process than a purely physical one. That reception is noteworthy because it shows theological readers recognizing the relevance of List’s higher-level picture for anthropology, even if List himself does not write as a theologian.

The pattern is clear. Anglo analytic reception often focuses on physicalism, supervenience, and causal exclusion. Spanish and French discussions sharpen the worry that levels may not truly save alternative possibilities. German discussion, including List’s own, emphasizes reasons, alternatives, and the indispensability of decision-theoretic explanation. The theological reception notices the anthropological payoff: List gives a way to defend human freedom without rejecting science.

20. The main objections to a Listian philosophy of mind

The first objection is the level-gap objection. Why should agential-level indeterminism be considered ontically real if the physical base is deterministic? Critics argue that higher-level openness may reflect limited description, not genuine possibility. List’s answer is that the relevant sciences use agential modal concepts, and if we are realist about those sciences, we should be realist about the alternatives they posit. But the objection remains: does explanatory indispensability guarantee ontological reality?

The second objection is the causal exclusion objection. List and Menzies answer it with proportional causation, but some physicalists will say this only changes causal description, not causal production. They may accept that mental explanations are useful while denying that mental properties add causal power. List’s reply is that causation itself should be understood in difference-making terms, not as a hidden physical push.

The third objection is the fragmentation objection. Many-worlds consciousness may seem metaphysically extravagant. Do we really need many first-personally centered worlds? List’s answer is that any theory must pay a price: reject first-person realism, embrace solipsism, allow fragmentation, or give up one-world metaphysics. His preferred price is abandoning one-world.

The fourth objection is theological. If every subject has a distinct first-personally centered world, does this fragment creaturely communion? A Listian response would say no: subjects still share a third-personal world and interact within it. The first-personal plurality explains privacy of experience, not social isolation.

The fifth objection concerns the soul. List’s framework may be too formal to capture the richness of soul-language: love, holiness, sin, prayer, grace, sanctification, and communion with God. That is true. List gives a metaphysical grammar, not a spiritual anthropology. Theology would need to add normative, relational, and soteriological content.

21. Extracted philosophy of mind: a systematic formulation

A comprehensive Listian philosophy of mind can be stated in the following theses.

First, reality is level-structured. Physical, biological, psychological, social, and first-personal facts may belong to different levels. Higher-level facts can be real without being reducible to lower-level facts.

Second, mental states such as beliefs, desires, preferences, and intentions are higher-level psychological states. They may supervene on physical states but are not identical to particular physical realizers.

Third, multiple realizability blocks simple reduction. The same mental or agential property may be realized by different neural or physical configurations.

Fourth, mental causation is real. Mental properties can be proportional difference-making causes of behavior. The fact that they are physically realized does not make them epiphenomenal.

Fifth, agency is an explanatory and ontological category. Agents are systems whose behavior is best understood in terms of goals, beliefs, reasons, options, and choices.

Sixth, free will is real at the agential level. It consists in intentional agency, alternative possibilities, and mental causal control. Physical determinism, if true, does not automatically remove agential alternatives.

Seventh, reasons require alternatives. To deliberate is to stand before possible actions with reasons for and against them. A theory that preserves reasons while eliminating genuine alternatives risks making deliberation merely theatrical.

Eighth, agency and phenomenal consciousness are distinct. A system may be an intentional agent without there being anything it is like to be that system. This is especially important for groups and AI.

Ninth, subjective experience is irreducibly first-personal. The fact that an experience is mine, present to me, or lived from here cannot be derived from a complete third-person physical description.

Tenth, physicalism fails for consciousness if physicalism means that all facts supervene on third-person physical facts. The third-person inventory omits first-person centeredness.

Eleventh, standard substance dualism also fails if it merely adds a nonphysical object to the third-person inventory. The problem is not only materiality; it is first-personal presence.

Twelfth, consciousness may require many first-personally centered worlds. Each conscious subject is associated with a centered world in which that subject’s experiences are present. The shared third-personal world is an abstraction or supervenient structure over these centered worlds.

Thirteenth, the self is the embodied higher-level agent centered by first-personal subjectivity. It is not reducible to the externally described organism, but neither is it shown to be a separable immortal substance.

Fourteenth, moral responsibility is grounded in agency, alternatives, and mental causation. Non-derivative moral status may require phenomenal consciousness, which distinguishes human persons and perhaps animals from merely institutional agents.

Fifteenth, theologically, the soul can be reconstructed as the first-personal and agential reality of the person. But immortality, resurrection, and divine relation require additional theological premises.

22. Final assessment

Christian List’s philosophy of mind is important because it refuses two simplifications. Against reductive physicalism, it insists that persons are not exhausted by third-person physical descriptions. Minds, agents, choices, and first-person experiences are real. Against naïve dualism, it insists that merely adding a nonphysical substance does not solve the deepest problem of consciousness. The real issue is first-personal presence.

The best concise characterization is this:

List offers a naturalistic emergent realism about agency and a first-personal anti-physicalism about consciousness.

That combination is philosophically unusual. It means that the person is both a higher-level agent and a first-personal center. The first aspect supports freedom, responsibility, intentionality, and rational action. The second supports inwardness, subjectivity, and the irreducibility of lived experience. Together, they yield a powerful contemporary analogue of the soul—but not a traditional proof of the soul as an immortal substance.

For theology, List is neither an ally to be used uncritically nor an opponent to be dismissed. His work disciplines theological soul-language. It suggests that the soul should not be conceived as a ghostly object hidden behind the body. It should be conceived, if at all, as the living person’s irreducible first-personal and agential reality: the person as subject, not merely object; as chooser, not merely mechanism; as bearer of experience, not merely organism.

That is the philosophy of mind one can responsibly extract from Christian List: a levelled, realist, anti-reductionist, first-personal philosophy in which mind is real, agency is causally efficacious, free will is a higher-level phenomenon, and consciousness forces metaphysics beyond the single objective world of standard physicalism.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
